title: Choosing an Initialization Strategy
concept: A practical initialization decision matrix is simple: avoid zero weights for hidden layers, use plain small random values as a basic symmetry breaker, prefer Xavier for Sigmoid/Tanh, and prefer He for ReLU-family networks.

exercises.prompt: Recap: If your hidden layers mainly use ReLU, which initialization is the usual default?
exercises.explain: Correct. Now let's turn the whole unit into a usable decision guide.

cards.title: Picking the right shoes for the trail
cards.body: You would not wear ice skates on a rocky hike or sandals in deep snow. Initialization is the same kind of choice: the best start depends on what kind of network and activation path you are about to walk.

cards.title: A simple decision matrix
cards.body: Use four buckets. Zero initialization is usually a bad choice for hidden weights because it keeps neurons identical. Plain small random initialization is the basic symmetry breaker. Xavier is the classic activation-aware choice for Sigmoid and Tanh. He is the classic activation-aware choice for ReLU-family layers.

cards.title: Packing for the weather
cards.body: If the forecast says rain, you pack a jacket; if it says snow, you pack boots. You do not bring every possible item and hope. Initialization choices work the same way: look at the activation family and choose the tool that fits that climate.

cards.title: Three sensible choices
cards.body: If you are building a shallow toy network and just need neurons to stop being clones, small random weights may be enough. If a hidden stack uses Tanh, Xavier is the better default. If a CNN or MLP uses ReLU or Leaky ReLU, He is the usual starting choice. What you should almost never do for hidden weights is start everything at zero.

cards.title: Newer-looking does not always mean better
cards.body: A common myth is that the most specialized-sounding initialization is automatically best. But the real rule is simpler: match the initialization to the activation and avoid known bad starts. The right fit beats the fanciest name.

cards.title: Bad default vs. matched default
cards.body: A tiny decision matrix goes a long way.

left.text:
Zero hidden weights: symmetry problem
Badly scaled random: unstable or fading signals
Wrong match for the activation

right.text:
Small random: basic symmetry break
Xavier: Sigmoid or Tanh
He: ReLU family

exercises.prompt: A hidden stack mainly uses Tanh activations. Which initialization is the best default match?

options.text: He only, because He is always best.

exercises.explain: Correct. Xavier is the classic activation-aware default for Sigmoid and Tanh networks.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Zero initialization is usually fine for hidden-layer weights in deep networks.
exercises.explain: False. Hidden neurons need different starting points so they can learn different roles.

exercises.sentence: For hidden layers built around ReLU, a common initialization choice is ___.
exercises.explain: Yes. He is the common default for ReLU-family networks.

options.text: Zero hidden weights: usually bad; Xavier: Sigmoid/Tanh; He: ReLU family.
Zero hidden weights: best general default; Xavier: only for output labels; He: only for datasets with images.
All four choices are interchangeable if the learning rate is small enough.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The safe rule is to avoid zero hidden weights and match Xavier or He to the activation family.

exercises.prompt: A teammate proposes three starts: all-zero hidden weights for a deep MLP, Xavier for a Tanh sentiment model, and He for a ReLU image model. Which evaluation is best?

options.text: Reject the all-zero hidden weights, keep Xavier for the Tanh model, and keep He for the ReLU model.
Keep all three, because hidden neurons learn different roles even when they all start at zero.
Replace Xavier and He with giant random weights so both models learn faster.
Use Xavier for every network, because activation type never matters.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The decision matrix is simple: avoid zero hidden weights, use Xavier for Sigmoid/Tanh style layers, and use He for ReLU-family layers.
